JB
PART I:
INTRODUCTION TO
THE STRAIGHT LINE SYSTEM

-- 1 of 7 --

t the core of The Straight Line Sales and Persuasion System is The Syntax
that defines each of 12-steps necessary to close a sale. It begins with the
first words spoken on the phone or the first eye contact in the case of a person-
to-person meeting.
A

-- 2 of 7 --

THE FIRST FOUR SECONDS
e’re hard-wired to be cautious, even fearful beings, and our unconscious
mind stands at the ready to keep us out of harm’s way. The brain reacts
quickly and decisively. Because of this you have no more than four seconds on the
phone and a miniscule one-quarter of a second person-to-person, to make a
positive impression. Obviously it takes more than a quarter second to say “hello.”
The point here is that before you have a chance to open your mouth your
prospect’s brain has already begun to size you up based on your appearance and
body language.
How you present yourself in that four-second time frame will make it or break for
you. You must establish that you are:
1) sharp as a tack
2) enthusiastic as hell
3) an expert in your field
Fail to do this in the first four seconds and you risk loosing control to your
prospect. Why? Because every prospect operates from a what’s-in-it-for-me frame
of mind. They want assurance you’re not wasting their time. If your prospect
senses this is happening, you’ll simply be dismissed. You’ve lost the opportunity
for a sale.
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com
W

-- 3 of 7 --

JORDAN BELFORT
If your prospect hangs on, he’ll start throwing questions at you, either to gain
some sense of clarity or out of some warped sense of wanting to sound polite.
Either way, you now have to take a reactive rather than a proactive stance. Your
prospect is now in control of the conversation. Any attempt to qualify your
prospect by asking questions will simply be met by more questions for you to
answer, various objections and other stall tactics. The whole situation has devolved
into utter chaos and a close will be all but impossible.
You’ve been branded a novice by your prospect’s brain. Pack your bags and go
home. Your prospect, just like you, wants to deal with an expert.
So how do you establish your expertise on the phone when your prospect can’t
even see you? Well, how do you communicate over the phone? With your voice!
And it’s the tonality of your voice that sets the stage for success or failure.
Now don’t confuse tonality with tone. They’re very different animals. Tone can
relate to either music or voice and is specific to sound pitch, quality, and strength.
She has a squeaky voice or he has a gravelly voice. Regardless of tone, both people
can be great closers.
Tonality is used exclusively in music and relates to the harmonic effect of being in
a particular key. So a singer who hasn’t warmed up before performing may sound
“off key.” Likewise, your presentation can sound off key if you don’t make proper
use of tonality.
By the way, appearance and body language together with tonality make up 90%
of human communication. The actual words you use make up the remaining
10%. So you can easily see what your prospect’s brain will focus on in that crucial
four seconds.
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 4 of 7 --

JORDAN BELFORT
Some examples of tonality …
When you want to share a secret with your best friend, you … whisper. That’s a
tonality and a visceral one at that. Not only does it build a sense of intrigue, it’s
very intimate. Not so much in the bedroom sense, but a whisper cannot be heard
from three feet away. So two people need to draw closer to share it.
In sales, however, you’re not likely to use the kind of “I love you” whisper you’d
share with your spouse. In sales you’ll use the power whisper. Just emphatic
enough so your prospect understands the seriousness of your words. And pays
attention!
At the same time you might lower your voice and slow the pace of your speech. It
becomes more deliberate. These are two more tonalities.
Combining tonalities is superbly effective.
Remember this: The words you speak are picked up by the conscious brain and
are weighed on a logical scale. How you say those words is picked up by the
unconscious brain and is weighed on an emotional scale.
Your enthusiasm is infectious!
When you speak with enthusiasm, your prospect’s ears perk up. Heart rate
increases. Pupils dilate. And an emotional certainty is created in your prospect’s
mind. The thought occurs, “this person really believes in his product, so it must
be good.”
Your enthusiasm is easily recognizable because you raise your voice and quicken
its pace. Two more tonalities. But you’re not screaming. Nothing over the top.
This is a bottled enthusiasm, which sits just below the surface and bubbles over as
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 5 of 7 --

JORDAN BELFORT
you speak. It’s about enunciating your words with absolute clarity, and stressing
your consonants, so your words convey a sense of intensity.
In total there are 29 tonalities we use every day. Ten of them are critical to closing
the sale. As you go through your day and converse with various people, see how
many you can identify.
Taking control of the sale
Use tonality to gain instant control of the conversation and to maintain your
prospect’s attention.
Control does not mean you dominate the conversation to the point your prospect
can’t get a word in edgewise. It does mean you set the pace and direction of the
conversation. It means you get to ask the questions that allow you to discover
your prospect’s logical needs and emotional wants and pain points.
That kind of control signals to your prospect that you are a true expert worthy of
his or her time. When you’re in control you’ll get honest answers to your
questions, making it much easier to address those needs, wants and pain points.
Control also means short-circuiting the most common objections by seeding the
answers to those objections into your presentation. You’re setting the stage for a
much easier close.
Control is at the very core of the Straight Line System. You take control the
moment you make eye contact and with the very first word you speak. You
maintain that control by establishing rapport and by asking qualifying questions,
nudging your prospect along the straight line until you close. The perfect sale.
And that rarely occurs. More likely, your prospects will have questions of their
own, plus objections, concerns and doubts. All of which will pull you and them
off that straight line.
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 6 of 7 --

JORDAN BELFORT
Keeping your prospect on the straight line will be the subject of upcoming
modules. So stay tuned. For now, here’s a quick review:
1. 	The core of The Straight Line Sales and Persuasion System is The Syntax that
defines each of 12-steps necessary to close a sale.
2. 	Step 1 is to establish that you're …
- 	Sharp as a tack
- 	Enthusiastic as hell
- 	An expert in your field
3. 	This must be accomplished within the first four seconds.
4. 	Enthusiasm creates an emotional certainty in your prospect's mind. The
thought occurs, “this person really believes in his product, so it must be
good.”
5. 	Enthusiasm is about enunciating your words with absolute clarity, and
stressing your consonants, so your words convey a sense of intensity. It's not
about being over the top.
6. 	Tonality is the device prospects use to size you up.
7. 	There are 29 tonalities we use every day.
8. 	Ten tonalities are critical to closing the sale.
9. 	Examples of tonality include:
- 	Whisper, especially the power whisper
- 	Lower your voice
- 	Raise your voice
- 	Slow the pace of your speech
- 	Increase the pace of your speech
10. Words relate to the conscious brain and are weighed on a logical scale.
11. The tonality of words relates the unconscious brain and is weighed on an
emotional scale.
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 7 of 7 --
